title: Resonance Frequency Shift Measurements of SRF Cavities at DESY
authors: Rezvan Ghanbari, Thorsten Buettner, Wolfgang Hillert, Karol Kasprzak, Tom Krokotsch, Ricardo Monroy-Villa, Detlef Reschke, Lea Steder, Alexey Sulimov, Hans Weise, Marc Wenskat, Mateusz Wiencek, Jonas Wolff
abstract: The variation of the resonance frequency and intrinsic quality factor of superconducting radio-frequency cavities during the transition from the superconducting to the normal-conducting state provides essential insight into the fundamental superconducting properties of the cavity material. Investigating these transition dynamics is crucial for the continued advancement of niobium cavities whose near-surface regions are intentionally modified through the controlled introduction of interstitial atoms, such as oxygen and nitrogen, leading to the emergence of several novel behaviors whose underlying mechanisms are not yet fully understood. This work reports on the development and commissioning of a dedicated frequency-shift measurement setup. In its initial implementation, the system establishes a precise framework for determining the electron mean free path within both the superconducting penetration depth and the normal-conducting skin depth. It further enables investigation of an anomalous dip in the temperature dependence of the frequency shift near the critical temperature in cavities containing interstitial atoms in the near-surface lattice, a novel phenomenon previously reported in the literature. A recent upgrade, currently in the final stage of validation, significantly improves measurement accuracy and reproducibility. The improved setup enables comprehensive studies of the frequency shift and quality factor over the full temperature range above 7 K, contributing to a deeper understanding of the superconducting properties.
summary: This paper describes the development of a high-precision measurement technique for studying the superconducting properties of niobium radio-frequency cavities. By implementing a system to correct for mechanical-stress-induced frequency drifts and thermal gradients, the researchers can accurately observe anomalous behaviors like the frequency 'dip' near the critical temperature. The work allows for the precise determination of the electron mean free path and oxygen concentration in the cavity surface layers. This is crucial for advancing the performance of SRF cavities used in particle accelerators.
main_problem: The paper addresses the need for precise measurement of resonance frequency shifts and quality factors in niobium SRF cavities, specifically mitigating systematic errors from mechanical stress and temperature gradients during the superconducting-to-normal transition.
main_result: The authors developed and validated a measurement setup and correction method that effectively removes frequency drifts caused by mechanical stress and reduces temperature gradient uncertainties, enabling reliable characterization of the anomalous frequency 'dip' and electron mean free path in mid-T heat-treated cavities.
method: The study uses a dedicated frequency-shift measurement setup in vertical cryostats at DESY, employing Lorentzian fitting of S21 transmission spectra and an empirical frequency-drift correction method during a controlled non-adiabatic warm-up.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of 1.3 GHz TESLA-type niobium superconducting radio-frequency (SRF) cavities, specifically comparing baseline cavities to those subjected to mid-temperature (300 C) heat treatments.
key_observables: Resonance frequency (f), loaded quality factor (QL), intrinsic quality factor (Q0), surface resistance (Rs), and penetration depth (lambda).
important_parameters: Critical temperature (Tc), electron mean free path (l), temperature gradients (grad T), and oxygen concentration (CO).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the expression for the relationship between penetration depth and mean free path remains constant across different heat treatments, and that the temperature dependence in the superconducting region can be effectively handled by using a fixed temperature range.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the experimental setup; Fig 2-3 demonstrate the effectiveness of Lorentzian fitting for frequency extraction; Fig 4 shows monitored parameters during warm-up; Fig 5-6 illustrate the empirical drift correction process; Fig 7 compares tightened vs loosened mounting screws; Fig 8-9 show the impact of heat treatment and temperature gradients on frequency and quality factor curves.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the scientific problem of characterizing Nb cavity transitions, describes the experimental setup and the development of the frequency-shift measurement framework, details the mitigation of systematic errors (mechanical stress and thermal gradients), presents the results of mid-T heat treatment characterization, and concludes with the derivation of physical parameters like mean free path and oxygen concentration.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Experimental setup for frequency-shift measure- ments. Liquid helium (LHe) is removed from the vessel im- mediately before the measurement. During warm-up, warm helium gas (GHe) is injected through the VC5110 valve, while the VC1210 return pipe is used for gas evacuation and pres- sure regulation. The VC1210 is originally part of the heat- exchange system, including the low-pressure heat exchanger (LPHX) and the Joule–Thomson (JT) valve, used for cool- ing below the helium boiling point at atmospheric pressure (∼4.2 K). A pressure sensor (P-sensor) is mounted inside the cryostat, and three CernoxTM temperature sensors (T-sensors 1, 2, and 3) are attached to the outer cavity surface....

FIG. 2. Accuracy enhancement of resonance-frequency analysis through Lorentzian fitting for an exemplary cavity. Example spectra: (a) below the critical temperature, exhibiting a low noise floor, and (b) above the critical temperature, showing an increased noise level. This elevation markedly increases the uncertainty in determining both the resonance peak frequency and the −3 dB bandwidth ∆f ′ H used for calculating QL. Applying a Lorentzian fit effectively mitigates the adverse effects of noise on both peak identification and bandwidth estimation. The extracted resonance frequencies and the corresponding loaded quality factors QL are shown in (c) and (d), respectively, obtained from...

FIG. 3. Two exemplary cavities, shown in (a) and (b), illustrate the importance of Lorentzian fitting for identifying and accurately measuring the resonance-frequency dip around the critical temperature in the cryogenic temperature T dependence of the resonance frequency for mid-T heat-treated cavities. Around and above the critical temperature, elevated noise levels obscure the accurate determination of the resonance frequency directly from the vector network analyzer (VNA). The insets show a zoom-in of the dip around the critical temperature.

FIG. 4. Monitored parameters during the cryostat warm-up for resonance-frequency-shift measurements: (a) Resonance fre- quency f and cryogenic temperature T of the cavity as a function of time during warm-up. A frequency drift at the onset of the measurements is observed, which, based on theory, is not due to the temperature dependence of the penetration depth and, consequently, the resonance-frequency shift. The unwanted frequency drift typically starts at the lowest measured tem- perature and gradually decreases at higher temperatures. (b) Corresponding pressure P read from the P-sensor, maintained at 1111 ± 0.16 mbar, and flow rates, each normalized to its respective maximum flow...

FIG. 5. Systematic empirical frequency-drift correction: (a) An unwanted frequency drift is observed at the onset of the frequency-shift measurement. (b) The frequency-drift slope is determined at the start of the measurement and at the residual drift level above critical temperature Tc. (c) The first linear fit at 7 K is extrapolated to match the slope of the second linear fit above Tc, in order to empirically estimate the frequency-drift curve. (d) The extrapolated frequency-drift curve is subtracted from the measured resonance frequency to obtain the corrected resonance-frequency shift. Consequently, only the corrected resonance-frequency shifts acquired above 7 K were included in the...

FIG. 6. Representative examples of post-measurement empirical frequency-drift corrections applied systematically to the data. Based on repeated measurements, the maximum relative uncertainties in the corrected frequency shifts (purple solid curves with data points and error bars) are estimated to be approximately 25% of the empirical frequency drifts (green dotted curves). A systematic temperature offset of approximately 0.11 K in the temperature range around Tc was identified between two T-sensor 2 positions at the cavity equators in two different inserts used for the measurements. This offset was corrected, and a corresponding systematic error of 0.11 K was included in the uncertainty...

FIG. 7. Three repeated frequency-shift measurements of the same cavity. One test was performed with the mount- ing screw connecting the cavity to the insert fully tightened (dashed curve), resulting in a frequency artifact. Two sub- sequent tests were conducted with the screws intentionally left slightly loose, as shown in the inset. This additional mechanical freedom relieved stress and reduced cavity de- formation caused by insert thermal expansion under tran- sient non-equilibrium conditions (see Fig. 4). Consequently, the frequency artifact disappeared, and the measurements with the screw loosely attached to the insert exhibited re- liable reproducibility, with an uncertainty of...

FIG. 8. Cryogenic temperature dependence of (a) resonance-frequency shift ∆f and (b) loaded quality fac- tor QL for an exemplary cavity before (1DE03 I) and after (1DE03 II) a mid-T heat treatment of 3 hours at 300◦C. The total frequency shift ∆fmax, critical temperature Tc, dip-onset temperature Tonset, and QL at 10 K are indicated and used in the analysis presented in this study. Due to the emergence of the dip around Tc, a quantitative definition of Tc in the fre- quency shift of mid-T heat-treated cavities requires further investigation. The differences in the curve features include a larger ∆f, a suppression of Tc, a dip around Tc, a lower QL above Tc, and a higher QL at temperatures...

FIG. 9. Effect of spatial temperature gradients ∇T on the critical temperature Tc and the dip-onset temperature Tonset in f(T) and QL(T) curves (see Fig. 8 for the definitions of Tc and Tonset). Such gradients hinder accurate determination of the true critical temperature, shifting it unidirectionally toward higher apparent values; the corresponding maximum uncertainties are represented as error bars. The dashed line indicates the critical temperature of niobium, 9.27 K. Our observations (currently under further investigation) show that covering around the cavity during frequency-shift measure- ments can mitigate direct exposure to the cryostat environ- ment, thereby minimizing temperature...

FIG. 10. Magnified temperature dependence of the resonance-frequency shift in the region of dip emergence around the critical temperature for a mid-T heat-treated cavity tested twice under different spatial temperature gra- dients. The effects of the spatial temperature gradient on the cavity-temperature readings and the corresponding uncer- tainties in the frequency shift are shown as error bars. These results confirm that, although the analysis of absolute tem- perature values is limited by high uncertainty, the influence of the spatial temperature gradient on the characterization of the dip—as a narrow relative feature—is negligible.
